content:
2 min read
Just now

--

In my last post, I talked about what real consistency actually looks like, not a perfect calendar, just someone who keeps returning to the work. This post is about the opposite process. The one that happens in reverse, so slowly that most people don’t realize it’s happening until it’s already done.
Most people think giving up is a single moment. You wake up one day and say "I quit."
I don’t think that’s how it usually happens. I think that’s some of the reason a lot of us stay stuck. Because we give up without ever noticing we did.
Here’s what giving up actually looks like.
You used to spend real time on your skill every single day. Then, one day, you catch yourself thinking it’s impossible to start again. That thought shows up quietly. It doesn’t announce itself as "giving up." It just feels like being tired, or busy, or realistic.
Even when you see someone else succeeding at the exact thing you paused on, it still doesn’t pull you back in. You’d think seeing proof it works would be motivating. Sometimes it isn’t. Sometimes it just confirms the story that they made it and you didn’t, so why try again.
That thought is the actual mechanism. Not a dramatic breakdown. Just a quiet decision to not start again today.
And it moves in stages. You go from working on your skill every day, to five times a week, without really deciding to. Then four times. Then twice. Then once a week. At some point, "once a week" quietly becomes "whenever I feel like it," which quietly becomes "not really anymore."
Nobody sends an announcement. There’s no final day marked on a calendar. Just a slow fade that only looks obvious in hindsight.
The good news is that the process runs in reverse too. The same way people fade from daily to nothing, you can build the opposite. Once a week becomes twice. Twice becomes four times. Four times becomes every day. And at some point, showing up stops being a decision you have to make and just becomes who you are.
That’s really the whole difference between people who make it in web dev, or any skill, and people who don’t. It’s rarely one dramatic moment of quitting or one dramatic moment of breaking through. It’s a thousand small, quiet choices, the kind that don’t feel significant on the day you make them.
If you’re in that quiet fade right now, the version where you haven’t officially quit but you also haven’t shown up in a while, that’s not a life sentence. It’s just the reverse staircase. And you can start climbing it the same way you came down. One small step, not the whole flight at once.
